(A) Glucose (Glc) and ATP is phosphorylated into glucose 6-phosphate (Glc-6-P) and ADP. Glc-6-P can be converted into glucose 1-phosphate (Glc-1-P). Glc-1-P and uridine triphosphate (UTE) is then converted to uridine diphosphate (UDP) glucose and pyrophosphate (PPi)
(B) Glycogen chains are elongated by the binding of a glucose residue to the free end of the glycogen chain, releasing UDP.
(C) Branching enzyme transfers chains of glucose residues to a new site, adding a new branch to the glycogen molecule. Glycogenolysis within the cytosol:
(D) Glc-1-P is released via a phosphoric reaction from the glycogen molecule until 4 terminal glucose residues remain. Glu-1-P is then converted into Glc-6-p and finally hydrolyzed into glucose and inorganic phosphate (Pi), see (A).
(E) 3 out of the 4 remaining glucose residues from a branch are transferred to a nearby branch, the remaining glucose residue is cleaved via hydrolysis releasing free glucose. Glycogenolysis within lysosomes:
(F) Glucose residues are cleaved via hydrolysis releasing free glucose.

ground_truth: Train-of-four stimulations are done by delivering four successive supramaximal stimuli (at 500 ms intervals) to a peripheral nerve. Those groups of four stimulations are repeatedly delivered to evaluate the resulting pattern of muscular response. Normally, the resulting muscular responses (as measured via electromyography) should be equally strong (both within groups of four consecutive responses and over a succession of consecutive groups of such responses). If nondepolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs (e.g., rocuronium) are administered, the amplitude of muscular responses decreases within a group of four consecutive responses (that the amplitude of the fourth response is significantly lower than that of the first one of the same group). This phenomenon is also referred to as “fade.” The effects of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs (e.g., succinylcholine) are subdivided into two phases: In phase I, the drug binds to the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, inducing a single, persistent depolarization. Electromyographically, each of the individual responses in a group of four will be decreased by the same percentage, and the overall muscular responsiveness between consecutive groups of stimuli steadily decreases over time. If depolarizing neuromuscular agents are administered over a prolonged amount of time or a high dose is given, phase II may occur. In this phase, there is a fade in the muscular response, resembling the phenomenon seen in nondepolarizing agents. Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., neostigmine) potentiate the effects of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking drugs during phase I.

Left ventricular and aortic pressures rise as the left ventricle ejects blood into the aorta. Pressures within the left ventricle and aorta are almost identical during this period. The aortic valve is still open during this phase. (D) Closure of the aortic valve as the left ventricular pressure falls below the aortic pressure. This marks the beginning of diastole (isovolumetric relaxation). (E) There is an increase in the aortic pressure (the dicrotic notch) that is caused by the closure of the aortic valve. Left ventricular pressure falls rapidly as the ventricles relax. Point E represents the highest pressure difference between the aorta and the left ventricle (aorta > left ventricle). It is responsible for the peak coronary blood flow at this phase. (F) The aortic pressure decreases gradually during ventricular relaxation. Due to its inherent vascular resistance, aortic pressures remain higher than left ventricular pressures during ventricular diastole.
